title: Spring fondant emergency feeding
subtitle: What to do when a colony is running short of stores before the spring flow arrives.
excerpt: April is a dangerous month for bees. The colony has been growing since February, stores have been consumed faster than in January, and the early flowers have not yet opened reliably. A colony that felt heavy in November can be light enough to starve by late March.
sourceNotes: National Bee Unit, Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024), spring management. BBKA Good Practice Guide on feeding bees. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: The gap between winter's end and the first reliable nectar flow, typically March to mid-April in the UK. Colonies are growing rapidly but forage is scarce or weather-locked. Many starvation losses happen in this window.

Placing a block of solid sugar paste (baker's fondant or purpose-made bee fondant) directly onto the top bars above the cluster. The bees consume it without adding water, making it suitable for cold weather when liquid syrup would chill the colony.

content.text: is the most predictable danger of the beekeeping year. Heft your hive on every visit from February onward. A hive that lifts noticeably easily needs feeding now, not after the next inspection.

In cold weather, use fondant only. Liquid syrup requires the bees to process moisture they cannot properly evaporate in a cold cluster; the resulting dampness chills the brood and increases the chance of chalkbrood. Fondant does not carry this risk. Baker's fondant from a supermarket or cash-and-carry works fine. Purpose-made bee fondant or pollen substitute fondant is better if you can source it, as it also addresses any early-season protein deficit.

attrs.title: When to switch to syrup
attrs.body: Once daytime temperatures are reliably above 14°C and flowers are open (typically mid-April to early May in southern England, later further north), switch to a heavy syrup (2 kg sugar to 1 L water) if stores are still low. Liquid syrup is easier for the bees to process in warm weather and cheaper than fondant at scale.

content.text: Choose a day above 8°C if possible. Wear a veil; a hungry colony in early spring can be more defensive than in summer. Lift the roof and set it aside. Remove the crown board with a smooth, steady motion, or crack it open with the hive tool and lift it. Slide a 500 g to 1 kg block of fondant directly onto the top bars above where the cluster sits; you can feel the warmth rising from the cluster through your glove. The fondant should rest directly on the bars so the bees can reach it from below without leaving the cluster.

Replace the crown board with an empty super or eke (a shallow wooden rim) underneath it to create space around the fondant. Replace the roof. The whole operation takes less than two minutes. A 1 kg block can sustain a small cluster for two to three weeks in early spring.

Check back in a week to see how much fondant has been consumed. If the block is down to crumbs, fit another. Once the blackthorn and willow are flowering and the colony's weight is stable or rising, stop supplementary feeding.
Using the rapid feeder in late spring

Once the weather warms, fit a rapid feeder and offer heavy syrup. Fit the feeder over the crown board hole at dusk to reduce robbing from neighbouring colonies. Fill it in the evening only; never leave a half-empty open feeder during the day. A rapid feeder can deliver 2 L of syrup in two days; check it before the bees empty it completely.
approach is a bridge to the first nectar flow; keep feeding until the colony no longer empties what you put in.
